content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Yesterday, I went to a part of town I was not familiar with. Google Maps was right there with me, guiding me turn by turn.
Then I hit a patch where coverage must have been sketchy. The map froze, and my location disappeared. For a moment, I did not know where I was or where my next turn was. I knew I needed to turn somewhere, so I did. A completely unfamiliar road, with unfamiliar names.
I knew I was lost.
But I would never admit that to myself, so I told myself I was exploring. It was exciting and a bit unsettling at the same time.
After a while, the blind patch must have ended, as Google Maps perked up and I knew my location in the world again. I felt relieved, but something else faded… my sense of wonder.
I was back in the world where everything is mapped, everything is known, everything is spoon-fed to me and there is nothing new to discover.
Our maps are incredible, and I am very thankful for them. But maybe… it’s time they evolved.
Maybe maps shouldn’t just tell us where things are.
Maybe they should tell us how places feel. The boring highway stretch compresses to a thin line, while the dense, magical market district blooms large and detailed. The map stops pretending to be objective truth and becomes a character that has opinions about where you are.
Maybe maps should let us choose the kind of journey we want: a simple slider that lets me pick how easy or discovery oriented I want my path to be. Depending on my selection, it could route me home through a street I’ve never taken or drop me two blocks early: “Find your own way from here.”
Maybe maps shouldn’t just show what is, but also what was.
Maybe they should have a layer you can turn on, where they superimpose and show the 1890 shoreline, the vanished streetcar line, the meadow that is now a parking lot, or a neighbor’s note that says:
“Best cherry tree in the city, third yard on the left.”
The map becomes something more than a tool.
It becomes a time machine.
Maybe maps need to evolve from the planet-as-surveyed to the planet-as-experienced.
We’ve mapped the world perfectly.
Now maybe it’s time to make it interesting again.
